content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 307 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance · · · Administration · · · Executive/Legal · · · Enforcement · · · Conflict of Interest
916 322-5862                322-5660              322-5901           322-6441              322-6444

September 27, 1979


                                                                                    A-99-158
Fred G. Wade
Supervisor Fourth District
Board of Supervisors
Sacramento County
700 H Street
Sacramento, CA  95814

Dear Mr. Wade:

     This letter is in response to your letter of June 23, 1979
and subsequent telephone conversations regarding the conflict
of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.  I hope
the following advice provided pursuant to Government Code Section
83114(b) is helpful.  The facts as you have provided them and
as I understand them are set forth below.

     You are a member of the Sacramento County Board of Super-
visors and the sole owner of the Wade Advertising Agency.  The
agency represents a number of clients some of whom require ad-
vertising space on billboards known as "bulletins".  It is
customary that when an advertising agency contracts for bulletin
space, it receives a commission of between 15 and 17% from the
bulletin company.  During the past twelve months your firm
has received more than $250 in commissions from Foster and
Kleiser, a nation-wide bulletin company.

     You have asked whether you should disqualify yourself
from making or participating in the making of decisions re-
garding the construction or removal of a bulletin owned by
Foster and Kleiser in Carmichael.

     The Political Reform Act provides in Government Code Section
87100,

     No public official at any level of state or local
government shall take, participate in making of any
way attempt to use his official position to influence
a governmental decision in which he knows or has
reason to know he has a financial interest.

Mr. Fred G. Wade                                           Page Two
September 27, 1979


     The term "financial interest" is defined in Section 87103
to mean,

          An official has a financial interest in a
     decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if
     it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
     will have a material financial effect, distin-
     guishable from its effect on the public generally,
     on:

          (c)  Any source of income, other than loans by
     a commercial lending institution in the regular
     course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty
     dollars ($250) or more in value received by or
     promised to the public official within twelve months
     prior to the time when the decision is made;. . .

     Section 82030 provides that income of an individual in-
cludes income received by a business entity in which the official
has a 10% or greater interest.  Since you are sole owner of
your advertising firm, Foster and Kleiser is a source of income
to you by virtue of the commissions it pays to your advertising
agency, and as it is clearly foreseeable that decisions con-
cerning the bulletin will affect Foster and Kleiser, the only
remaining question is whether the effects of the decision will
be material.

     Commission Regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 13702(a)
provides that the effects of a decision are material if they
will have a significant effect on a source of income.  2 Cal.
Adm. Code Section 13702(b)(1) and (3) of the regulation states
that the effect of a decision on a business entity which is a
source of income generally is material if the effect of a
decision will be to increase or decrease the amount of income
received by an official by $100 or more or increase or decrease:

          (A)  The annualized gross revenues by the lesser of:

               1.  One hundred thousand dollars ($100,000); or

               2.  One percent if it is one thousand dollars
          ($1,000) or more; or

          (B)  Annual net income by the lesser of:

               1.  Fifty thousand dollars ($50,000); or

Mr. Fred G. Wade                                                Page Three
September 27, 1979


                2. One half of one percent if it is one
        thousand dollars ($1,000) or more; or

        (C) Current assets or liabilities by the lessor
of:
                1. One hundred thousand dollars ($100,000);
        or
                2. One half of one percent if it is one
        thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

                Current assets are deemed to be decreased
        by the amount of any expenses incurred as a
        result of a governmental decision.

        2/ Cal. Adm. Code Section 19702(b)(1)(A), (B) & (C)

        Bulletins in the Sacramento area generally rent for $1300 per
month per side. Thus, it is annualized gross revenues to Foster and
Kleiser from the operation of the bulletin is approximately $31,000
This figure falls short of both the absolute and percentage
thresholds set forth in the regulation. In addition, it is my
understanding that the decision concerning the bulletin in question
will not affect the amount of your income nor will you receive
any preference or benefit from Foster and Kleiser as a result of
the decision.2/


___________________________________________________________

1/ For example, the annual report for Metromedia, which owns
Foster and Kleiser, states that Foster and Kleiser received gross
revenues in excess of $80,000,000 last year. If of $90 million is
$900,000 which is approximately 27 times higher than the annual
gross revenues that will be generated by the Carmichael bulletin.

2/ When construction on the Carmichael bulletin was completed,
Foster and Kleiser had not sold advertisements for both sides.
Shortly before the completion of construction, the contract for
bulletin advertisement between Foster and Kleiser and Capitol
Federal Savings and Loan Association (one of your firm's clients)
expired. Foster and Kleiser did not want any vacant space to appear
on the newly constructed bulletin. Therefore, until the space
could be sold, they displayed the recently expired Capitol Federal
Savings advertisement in the unsold space. This was done without
your knowledge or consent and within 48 hours after you learned
of the Capitol Federal Savings display, you ordered Foster and
Kleiser to have it removed immediately. Under these circumstances
we do not think that the brief appearance of the Capitol Federal
Savings advertisement alters the conclusions stated above.

Mr. Fred G. Wade
September 27, 1979

156
Page Four

On the basis of this information, we conclude that you are
not disqualified from making or participating in the making of
decisions regarding Foster and Kleiser's Carmichael bulletin.2/

If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate
to contact me.

Sincerely,

Ted Prim, Chief
Conflicts of Interest Division

Enclosure
TP:nrw


2/ If Foster and Kleiser were to seek several permits in the
County during the year, our conclusion might be different.

79158                                           '19 U0T

                                                  Request No.
                                                  Date Received 2-3-79
                                                  Response Due 2/12/79
                                                  PROPOSED RESPONSE
                                                  DUE: 2/1-79
                                                  FRED G. WADE
                                                  SUPERVISOR FOURTH DISTRICT
                                                  TELEPHONE (916) 440-5491

BOARD OF SUPERVISORS
COUNTY OF SACRAMENTO
700 H STREET           /      SACRAMENTO, CA. 95814


June 28, 1979


Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Attention: Ted Prin
           Conflict of Interest Division

           Re:  Request for Opinion Pertaining to Conflict of Interest

Gentlemen:

I represent the Fourth Supervisorial District as a member of the Board
of Supervisors of Sacramento County.  I have been a member of the Board
of Supervisors since 1975.

Certain members of the community have raised the question as to whether
I possessed a financial interest, and should have disqualified myself
from voting upon, a zoning matter which was recently acted upon by the
Board of Supervisors.  In my opinion, I did not possess a financial
interest which would have disqualified me from voting.  However, it is
important for me to know for purposes of future actions whether the
circumstances surrounding the transaction upon which I voted required
me to disqualify myself in the opinion of the FPPC.  If it should be
determined that those circumstances constitute a disqualification, I
will be better able to identify circumstances which require disqualifi-
cation in the future.

For the foregoing reasons I am requesting an opinion by the Fair Politi-
cal Practices Commission as to whether I possessed a financial interest
which required me to disqualify myself from voting upon the transaction
described below.

Toward the end of 1978, the firm of Foster and Kleiser applied to the
County of Sacramento for a conditional use permit under the County's
Zoning Ordinance to construct a billboard on a particular parcel of pro-
perty.  On February 26, 1979, the application was considered by the
Project Planning Commission during a public hearing.  The Planning Com-
mission denied the application.  Foster and Kleiser appealed the denial

Fair Political Practices Commission
Attention: Ted Prim
June 28, 1979
Page 2

(as is permitted by the Zoning Ordinance) to the Board of Supervisors.
On April 12, 1979, the Board of Supervisors considered the appeal during
a public hearing. At the close of the hearing the Board reversed the
decision of the Project Planning Commission, and granted the use permit
for the billboard, subject to various conditions. The vote by which the
permit was issued was three in favor and two against. My vote was one
of the three which was in favor.

Foster and Kleiser is a national outdoor advertising company, which does
business in Sacramento County. A segment of its business operations in-
volves the acquisition of sites adjacent to roadways, and the construction
thereon of billboards. The company sells space on the billboards to
private and public enterprises for advertising purposes. The sign mes-
sages are presented in behalf of the enterprises which purchase the space,
and Foster and Kleiser implements the message on the billboard by paint-
ing or posting screened paper.

Foster and Kleiser promulgates a standardized series of rates for the
purchase of billboard space. These rates are published, and applied
uniformly by Foster and Kleiser within Sacramento County to all prospec-
tive purchasers of space.

There are at least two other outdoor advertising companies which do
business in Sacramento County. These are National and Eller. Their
marketing practices are substantially similar to those of Foster and
Kleiser, described above.

I am the owner of an advertising agency, which is located in and does
business in Sacramento County. The agency is incorporated, and I am the
sole stockholder. My agency provides advice to its clientele concerning
promotional and advertising matters, develops advertising strategy,
creates copy and designs layout, and arranges for the placement of the
advertising with media. A small portion of the agency's media placement
consists of arrangement for billboard space.

When arranging for outdoor advertising space, the agency contacts firms
such as Foster and Kleiser, submits the layout, specifies the size and
number of billboards which the agency's client desires to purchase, and
otherwise makes arrangements for contracts, billings, etc. A contract
is entered into between the agency's client and the outdoor advertising
company. My agency signs the contract in the name of the client.

My agency's income is derived from two principal sources. The first is
fees from clients which are charged primarily on a time and material
basis, for such services as creating copy and designing layout. The
second is commissions as a result of placement of advertising with such
media as radio and television stations.

Fair Political Practices Commission
Attention: Ted Prim
June 28, 1979
Page 3

The standard rates for billboard space charged by Foster and Kleiser
are charged uniformly to all persons, businesses and entities which
place advertising with that company. However, if advertising is placed
by an advertising agency in behalf of a client, a 16-2/3% discount from
the standard rate is granted by Foster and Kleiser. The 16-2/3% dis-
count is retained by the advertising agency as a commission. Thus, the
client pays to the advertising agency the standard fee imposed by Foster
and Kleiser, the advertising agency retains 16-2/3% of that amount, and
remits the balance to Foster and Kleiser as payment for the billboard
space. The contract between the client and Foster and Kleiser provides
for the discount rate and commission retention by the advertising agency.
I understand that if an advertising agency is not recognized or does not
have an established credit rating, Foster and Kleiser requires that the
fee be paid directly by the client, and therein remits the 16-2/3% discount
to the advertising agency. The contract makes the client, and not the
advertising agency, liable for the fee.

To my knowledge the 16-2/3% discount rate is standard throughout the
billboard industry, with one exception. National Advertising Company
has established a discount rate of only 15%.

To the best of my knowledge neither Foster and Kleiser nor any other
billboard company varies its standard fees or percentage of discount
based upon volume of business which a client or advertising agency does
with the billboard company. Nor, to my knowledge, does Foster and
Kleiser or any other billboard company grant any preference in location
of placement of billboard advertising based upon volume of business
which a client or advertising agency does with that company.

On April 12, 1979, when I voted on the zoning matter described above,
my agency had three clients with existing contracts with Foster and
Kleiser for outdoor advertising space. One of those clients was Capital
Federal Savings and Loan. The contract which Capital Federal Savings
and Loan had with Foster and Kleiser expired June 2, 1979, and was not
renewed. Construction of the billboard authorized by the approval on
April 12, 1979, was completed sometime around the end of May or beginning
of June, 1979. Sometime after the billboard was completed, Foster and
Kleiser, without my request, consent or knowledge, and so far as I know,
without that of Capital Federal Savings and Loan (which had recently
been removed from another billboard as a result of expiration of contract)
on the new billboard which had been approved. The date of
installation is unknown. I saw the display on June 13, 1979. The dis-
play was replaced shortly thereafter.

I presume, though I do not know, that Foster and Kleiser made the in-
stallation of the Capital Federal Savings and Loan advertising for the

Fair Political Practices Commission .
Attention: Ted Prim
June 28, 1979
Page 4


purpose of covering up blank space on the new billboard until the
billboard could be covered with sold space.

During the period April, 1978, through April, 1979, my agency received
more than $250 in commissions from clients as a result of advertising
placed with Foster and Kleiser. My proprietary interest in the adver-
tising agency would be valued at more than $1,000. I have no investment
in Foster and Kleiser and no relationship with that firm, except as
described above.

Briefly stated, my belief that I did not possess a financial interest
which disqualified me from voting is based on the fact that the source
of the commission is the fee paid by my agency's client to Foster and
Kleiser. My agency purchases, in behalf of named clients, billboard
space from Foster and Kleiser. The fees for such space are standard-
ized, as is the discount which becomes my agency's commission retained
from the fees paid by the client. I have no interest in Foster and
Kleiser, financial or otherwise. The gratuitous placement by Foster
and Kleiser of my client's advertising on the newly constructed bill-
board was, to the best of my knowledge, a coincidental product of
Foster and Kleiser's desire to cover a newly constructed billboard and
the availability of a recently removed display as a result of expira-
tion of that client's contract with Foster and Kleiser.

Please do not hesitate to call me if you need additional information or
wish to discuss the facts relating to this request.

Sincerely,

FRED G. WADE

sections.question: Whether you should disqualify yourself from making or participating in the making of decisions regarding the construction or removal of a bulletin owned by Foster and Kleiser in Carmichael, given that your advertising agency receives commissions from Foster and Kleiser for arranging billboard advertising space.
sections.conclusion: On the basis of this information, we conclude that you are not disqualified from making or participating in the making of decisions regarding Foster and Kleiser's Carmichael bulletin.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a County Supervisor with a sole-owned advertising agency that receives commissions from Foster and Kleiser have a disqualifying financial interest in decisions regarding Foster and Kleiser's billboard construction permit?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Supervisor is not disqualified from participating in decisions regarding the Foster and Kleiser bulletin because the financial effect would not be material under applicable regulations, as the bulletin's annualized gross revenues fall below materiality thresholds and the Supervisor would receive no direct benefit from the specific decision.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Complete advice letter with question from incoming request and FPPC conclusion. Contains detailed legal analysis citing Government Code sections and administrative regulations. Minor OCR artifacts in document but key information clearly extracted. Includes incoming request letter (June 28, 1979) followed by FPPC response (September 27, 1979).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether you should disqualify yourself from making or participating in the making of decisions regarding the construction or removal of a bulletin owned by Foster and Kleiser in Carmichael, given that your advertising agency receives commissions from Foster and Kleiser for arranging billboard advertising space.

CONCLUSION: On the basis of this information, we conclude that you are not disqualified from making or participating in the making of decisions regarding Foster and Kleiser's Carmichael bulletin.

You are a member of the Sacramento County Board of Super- visors and the sole owner of the Wade Advertising Agency. embedding.summary: FPPC advises Sacramento County Supervisor Fred G. Wade that he is not disqualified from voting on a Foster and Kleiser billboard permit despite his sole ownership of an advertising agency that receives commissions from Foster and Kleiser, because the financial effects of the specific decision would not be material under Political Reform Act standards.
